ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Cholar Dal Narkel Diye; mapped to the closest parent class 'lentil dish' (FOODON_03311737) as a defensible approximate anchor. Chana dal (Bengal gram / Cicer arietinum split) is the primary ingredient.
ontology.foodex2_description: Legume-based dish (cooked lentils/pulses) — FoodEx2 level-3 category for cooked pulse dishes; used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for chana dal preparations.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Cholar Dal Narkel Diye. A00GJ (cooked legume/pulse dish) is the closest defensible category-level anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Bengali lentil preparation. SNOMED CT coverage of regional South Asian dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Cholar Dal Narkel Diye is approximately 250 g, typically served as a main lentil course alongside rice or luchi (fried flatbread) in a Bengali meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN (2017 revised edition); supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for coconut and spice components
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for the composite dish Cholar Dal Narkel Diye. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked chana dal (Bengal gram, boiled) as the primary base, with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for fresh coconut (NDB 12104) used to account for the coconut component (~15% by weight). Spice contributions to macronutrients are minor and consistent with ICMR-NIN spice tables. The input record values (energy 152 kcal, protein 7.2 g, fat 6.0 g, carbohydrate 18.0 g per 100 g) are consistent with this adapted composition and are retained. Sodium at 260 mg/100 g reflects added salt during cooking and is plausible for a restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database entry for chickpeas / Bengal gram (boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite dish Cholar Dal Narkel Diye. GI of 36 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled chickpeas / Bengal gram (chana dal), which is the dominant carbohydrate-contributing ingredient (GI range 28–45, central estimate 36). Fresh coconut and spices contribute negligible additional carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (36 × 18.0 g × 2.5) / 100 = 16.2 g per 250 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 260 mg/100 g (650 mg per 250 g serving) is borderline-high for a single dish; dietary fibre at 4.0 g/100 g supports blood pressure management.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation to bring sodium below 200 mg/100 g, and limit portion to 150–200 g; the high dietary fibre content (4.0 g/100 g) and estimated potassium from chana dal and coconut provide partial offset consistent with DASH diet principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~36) from chana dal (Bengal gram), high dietary fibre (4.0 g/100 g), and moderate protein (7.2 g/100 g) collectively slow postprandial glucose absorption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cholar Dal Narkel Diye is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when prepared without added sugar and with minimal ghee; limit serving to 150–200 g as part of a balanced meal to manage glycemic load, and substitute ghee with a small amount of vegetable oil to reduce saturated fat that may worsen insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.6 g/100 g (9.0 g per 250 g serving) from coconut and ghee is the primary cardiovascular concern; dietary fibre at 4.0 g/100 g and plant-based protein are cardioprotective.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with vegetable oil instead of ghee and use fresh coconut in moderation to reduce saturated fat intake; the high dietary fibre and low GI profile of chana dal are beneficial for LDL cholesterol management and overall cardiovascular risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) used in material quantities; saturated fat at 3.6 g/100 g is below the 5 g/100 g threshold; dietary fibre supports gut microbiome immune regulation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cholar Dal Narkel Diye is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory spice profile and high fibre content; those with nightshade sensitivity should monitor green chilli consumption, and ghee should be substituted with vegetable oil to further reduce saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cholar Dal Narkel Diye (Bengali: ছোলার ডাল নারকেল দিয়ে) is a traditional Bengali lentil dish made by slow-cooking chana dal (split Bengal gram, Cicer arietinum) with pieces of fresh coconut, tempered with whole spices including cumin seeds, bay leaf, dried red chilli, and finished with ginger paste, turmeric, and green chillies. The dish is typically prepared by first boiling the chana dal until tender but not mushy, then adding fresh coconut pieces and a spiced ghee or oil tempering (chaunk). The result is a mildly sweet, nutty, and aromatic lentil curry with a thick, semi-dry consistency. Cholar Dal Narkel Diye is a festive staple in Bengali Hindu cuisine, traditionally served during Durga Puja, weddings, and special occasions alongside luchi (deep-fried flatbread) or rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 152 kcal, 7.2 g protein, 6.0 g fat (3.6 g saturated from coconut and ghee), 18.0 g carbohydrate, and 4.0 g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 36 (low GI), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium at 260 mg/100 g is borderline for hypertension management and should be moderated. The anti-inflammatory spices turmeric and ginger add functional health value. This dish is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free, and is widely available in Indian restaurants in Singapore and across South and Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: What is Cholar Dal Narkel Diye?

Can I eat Cholar Dal if I have high cholesterol?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked chana dal (Bengal gram) supplemented by USDA FoodData Central data for fresh coconut, as no single approved-source entry exists for the composite dish Cholar Dal Narkel Diye. The input record values are consistent with this adapted composition and are retained. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled Bengal gram as the closest analogous food. Confidence is medium because no exact dish-level measurement from an approved primary source is available.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Bengali lentil-coconut dish is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite preparation is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level; estimated ~320 mg/100 g from component analysis (chana dal ~340 mg/100 g cooked, coconut ~356 mg/100 g per USDA) but not confirmed at dish level and therefore set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules. 3. water_g and remaining micronutrients (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1 through vitamin_b12, folate, vitamin_d, vitamin_e, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium) are set to -1: not reported for this composite dish in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this plant-based preparation with no hydrogenated fats; consistent with ICMR-NIN and USDA data for the component ingredients. 5. The input record listed food_group as 'Vegetables'; this has been corrected to 'Meat and alternatives' as chana dal is a legume/pulse and the dish is a protein-contributing lentil preparation. 6. The input ethnic_tags ('Curry', 'Biryani', 'Naan') were inaccurate for this dish and have been replaced with accurate classification. 7. country_of_origin in the input was listed as 'Singapore'; this has been corrected to 'West Bengal, India' as the dish's culinary origin; Singapore is the serving market context. 8. Vegan flag is set to false as traditional preparation uses ghee (clarified butter); vegetarian flag is true. 9. GI source is analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled Bengal gram (GI ~36); no direct measurement for the composite dish exists in approved sources.
